title: Vanillekipferl
subtitle: Austrian vanilla crescent cookies with ground almonds
excerpt: Small, crescent-shaped cookies made from a ground almond shortbread dough, rolled in vanilla icing sugar while still warm so the sugar clings and sets in a powdery coat. Austrian and German Christmas baking at its most characteristic. The dough is fragile; the shaping is done entirely by hand.
sourceNotes: Vanillekipferl are one of the oldest documented Viennese Christmas biscuits, with recipes appearing in Austrian and German domestic cookbooks throughout the nineteenth century. The almond-enriched shortbread dough and the vanilla sugar coating are consistent across all documented versions. Recipes appear in Katharina Prato, Die süddeutsche Küche (first edition 1858; numerous later editions), and in various Austrian confectionery manuals of the same period. The method here follows the established formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled Vanillekipferl before rolling in icing sugar. Thaw at room temperature, then roll in icing sugar.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Bake in batches of 20 at a time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a tin for up to two weeks. The cookies improve after a day as the icing sugar softens slightly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 140°C. Vanillekipferl should remain pale — the edges may take on the faintest colour but the tops should not brown. They are done when the underside is lightly gold and the cookie holds its shape when lifted.

glossaryTerms.definition: Each Vanillekipferl is shaped by rolling a small piece of chilled dough between your palms into a short log, thicker in the middle and tapered at each end, then curving the ends to form a crescent. The dough is crumbly and must be cold to hold together during shaping; if it cracks, press the dough back together and work quickly. The crescents are fragile until fully baked and cool.

content.text: Vanillekipferl are shaped entirely by hand — no rolling pin, no cutters. The challenge is the fragility of the almond dough, which has very little gluten to hold it together. Keep the dough cold between batches and use
to form each crescent quickly. They are rolled in vanilla icing sugar immediately from the oven, while hot, so the sugar melts slightly onto the surface.

Rub the cold butter into the flour, almond flour, icing sugar, vanilla, and salt until the mixture comes together into a crumbly dough. Squeeze the dough gently to check it holds together when compressed — it should. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 160°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper.

Pinch off pieces of chilled dough the size of a large grape — about 15 g each. Roll each piece between your palms into a short log, tapered at each end, roughly 6 cm long. Place on the lined tray and curve the ends gently into a crescent. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the underside is lightly gold and the cookies hold their shape when the tray is tilted.

Mix the rolling icing sugar with the vanilla in a shallow bowl. While the crescents are still hot, lift them carefully with a palette knife and roll gently in the vanilla icing sugar until coated on all sides. Transfer to a rack to cool. Sift extra icing sugar over the top once they are completely cool.
